People who live comfortably should be pitied if they are not ready for hardship. No one should consider themselves improved any more than they are made holy. The account of Tyre’s trade shows that God watches people even when they are involved in worldly business, not only when at church, praying and listening, but also when in markets and fairs, buying and selling. In all our dealings we should keep a conscience free from wrongdoing. God, as the common Father of humankind, makes one country plentiful in one product, and another in another, useful for the needs or comfort and decoration of human life. See what a blessing trade and commerce are to people when pursued with reverence for God. Besides necessities, many things become valuable only by custom; yet God allows us to use them. But when wealth increases, people tend to set their hearts on it and forget the Lord who gives power to gain riches.

Even the most powerful and impressive kingdoms and nations eventually decline. Those who trust in people and place their hopes in them will fall with them: blessed are those who have the God of Jacob for their help, and whose hope is in the Lord their God, who lives forever. Those involved in trade should learn to conduct their business according to God’s word. Those who possess wealth should remember they are the Lord's managers, and should use his possessions to do good to everyone. Let us seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness.
